text: Releasing the inferior pulmonary ligament makes a very good starting point.
It's best to start with the easiest group and build up the level of complexity as you go along.
It is imperative to know the surrounding anatomy in extreme detail and identify caveats and potential areas of danger.
Releasing the inferior pulmonary ligament exposes station 9 nodes and further opening of the pleura over the oesophagus and down and up to the origin of the diaphragm exposes station 8 nodes.
Dissection of these two groups of nodes is usually straightforward.
Our method of dissection evolved into using a malleable diaphragm spatula
insulated albeit for the last one or two millimeters whilst keeping the energy level at low.
Dissection in this manner is precise, hemostatic and reduces lymphatic leak.
On the right hand side, the subcarinal space is accessed between the right main bronchus and the ozygous vein.
The mediastinal pleural reflection at the back of the hilum is open longitudinally up to the concavity of the ozygous vein.
identifying the vagus nerve running medial to the vein and lateral to the oesophagus.
The subcarina nodes are dissected of their blood supply, taking care not to injure the membranous part of the right main bronchus.
Dissection is carried upwards until the carina and the left main bronchus are clearly identified.
Care must be exercised in labeling nodes appropriately, as parasophageal station 8 and parbronchial station 10 are easily mislabeled as station 7.
By the end of this dissection, the left membrane bronchus, carina, esophagus, and the back of the pericardium
This block of tissue can be accessed within what I call here the superior triangle.
The triangle is limited by two nerves, the vagus and the phrenic, and based on the ozygous vein.
A trapdoor is opened in the mediastinal pleura to expose the fibrofatty nodal block.
Once this is completed, the vagus could be identified stuck to the inside of the pleural reflection.
Slinging the vagus and the pleura over the superior vena cava can be helpful in opening the space.
Dissection is started at the border of the superior vena cava by pushing the superior vena cava away from the block.
One should be aware of the existence of at least one constant vein that drains directly into the SVC.
It is recommended to control this draining vein by lager clips before division.
If accidentally cut, these veins have the propensity to retract and disappear, making control of bleeding difficult.
Dissection is then deepened until the main stem trachea is identified.
Strands of tissue seen in this triangle are vagal branches to the trachea and the fibro fatty nodal block.
The small vessels seen are branches of bronchial arteries, some contributing to the lateral vascular mantle of the trachea.
The precarinal station 3 nodes on the right side are considered to be N2 nodes, and hence the dissected block of tissue should be labeled as stations 2 to 4.
After removal of the block, the bed of this space comes into light, where immediately the art of the aorta and origin of the brachiocephalic artery are clearly identified.
It is possible to dissect the highest station 2 nodes, but again extreme caution should be exercised.
A combination of blunt and diathermy dissection is usually required, helped by zooming in to get the maximum magnification of the field.

Caution should be exercised with diathermy at the apex of the superior triangle, especially at the origin of the right subclavian artery, as this is the likely location where the recurrent laryngeal nerve could be encountered.
Station 4 nodes sometimes exist separately at the tracheobronchial junction under the ozygous vein.
Dissection of hilar and interlobal nodes station 10 and 11 is performed during or after lung resection.
It might be necessary sometimes to remove station 11 nodes to skeletonize vessels to be able to control them using a stapling device.
The entire length of the main stem trachea is laid bare of nodes.
and the subcoronary space is checked one last time before ending the procedure.
In the left chest, systematic nodal dissection is started similarly by releasing the inferior pulmonary ligament.
Station 9 nodes are found within the ligament and around the inferior pulmonary vein.
The medial mediastinal pleura is opened as before to expose the parasophageal nodes, station 8, care being taken not to injure the oesophagus or the vagus nerve.
Again, the number and consistency of the fibro fatty nodal tissue may vary greatly here.
Nodes may be completely absent, discrete, or contained within a dense block of fatty tissue.
Removal of the subcrinal nodes, station 7 on the left side, is the most taxing and time consuming of all groups.
Understanding the normal relationship of the different structures around the space is the key to successful harvesting of station 7 nodes.
Note the recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks around the ligamentum arteriosum below the concavity of the arch of the aorta, the right-hand side of the vagus in this view.
This means that all the pulmonary vagal branches to the left can be cut with impunity.
Again, there are constant bronchial arteries arising directly from the aorta.
These should be identified and secured with liger clips before dividing.
The principle here is to follow the left main bronchus proximally until it leads us to the carina and to the right main bronchus.
Therefore, the lower lobe bronchus is dissected all round and a tape is passed around it for retraction to the left hand side of the operative field.
Likewise, a red sloop
or a Jake's catheter is passed around the oesophagus, which is retracted to the right hand side of the operative field.
This is particularly helpful in exposing the subcarinal space, while it's not cluttering the posterior port with operating instruments for retraction and dissection.
The subcarinal nodes are dissected using the same technique of pinpoint diathermy, while it's exercising care not to injure the membranous part of the bronchus.
By the end of this dissection, the carina and the right main bronchus, as well as the posterior pericardium and the oesophagus are well on display.
Nodes within the pleural reflection of the lung are labeled as station 10, whereas the most lateral of the aortopulmonary group at the tracheobronchial junction are labeled as station 4.
The pre-tracheal station 3 nodes are the most difficult to access and represent N3 nodes on the left side.
To expose this group of nodes, the main pulmonary artery is dissected off the bronchus, a red sloop passed around it, and the artery retracted to the right hand side of the operative field.
The space is followed proximally until the main stem trachea is identified.
Any nodal tissue anterior to this carinal position would constitute station 3 nodes.
As always, discrete feeding vessels should be controlled by a liger clip before removing the node.
Dissection of stations five and six starts by opening the front hilar pleura over the superior pulmonary vein.
The triangle made by the phrenic nerve and the vagus and aortic arch bears resemblance to the superior triangle on the right side, which is also limited by the same two nerves.
The block of tissue is lifted off the main pulmonary artery into the aortopulmonary space.
consistency of the fibrofatty nodal tissue also differ here greatly and in the majority of cases it is possible to remove station 5 and 6 arm block.
It's important to keep dissection to the left hand side of the vagus in this view to avoid injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The phrenic nerve could be sloped and retracted for better dissection but this is not mandatory.
All the tissue in the aorta pulmonary space superficial to the ligamentum arteriosum is dissected and blocked if possible.
Dissection is carried over the arch up to the origin of the left subclavian artery.
Hemostasis is secured.
